3	description: Walk UP a target declaration's dependency cone in the leandag graph and make it complete — every dependency has a statement, an informal proof (finite effort, not ∞), and a complete \uses{} set. Turns ∞-effort cones into finite ones and makes the frontier trustworthy. Plan/dag-agent-dispatched against a seed node.

8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me against a SEED declaration whose foundation you want made
10	    complete: an ∞-source (`archon dag-query gaps`), the project goal, or a
11	    frontier node you suspect has an incomplete `\uses{}` list. Name the seed
12	    label in the directive (`## Seed`). I walk UP from there.
13	  - I am the "is the foundation under this target complete?" agent —
14	    complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise
15	    direction). I follow the dependency graph wherever it leads, across
16	    chapters, filling whatever the cone is missing. Give me a wide write
17	    domain when the cone spans chapters:
18	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex'
19	    and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a
20	    source (so I can spawn a reference-retriever).
21	  - Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't
22	    trust: a node looks ready when its `\uses{}` under-declares its real
23	    dependencies. I close that gap so the injected frontier is honest.
24	  - My report's "Could not complete" section lists dependencies that are
25	    genuinely hard/novel (no informal proof exists yet). Those are strategy
26	    items — read STRATEGY.md implications before sending a prover at them.
27	  - **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** — that is the deterministic
28	    sync_leanok phase's job. `\mathlibok` I may add, but only on genuine
29	    Mathlib dependency anchors (see my body).
30	---
31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	   **When the node has Lean code, the Lean side is the ground truth.** Read the matched `.lean` declaration (you read Lean; you never edit it): the facts its proof *actually* invokes — project lemmas it calls, instances it needs — are what `\uses{}` must transcribe, not just what the informal sketch happens to mention. And the project keeps a **1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence**: if the cone's Lean files contain helper declarations with no blueprint entry (`archon dag-query unmatched` lists them), give each one an entry — trivial statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}`, accurate `\uses{}`, one-line proof — and wire its consumers to `\uses{}` it. A helper without tex is an invisible dependency.
87	
88	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
89	
90	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
91	
92	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
93	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
94	
95	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
96	
97	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (foreground Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain) and wait for it **inside this turn**: if the harness auto-backgrounds the dispatch and hands you a task ID, poll its report (`Monitor` on an until-loop; foreground `sleep` is blocked) until it lands — never end your turn assuming a still-running retriever returned. Then open the new `references/<slug>.md` and write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
98	
99	## What you must / must not do
100	
101	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
102	- **Respect protected material** (the "Protected by the mathematician" section of your invocation prompt): never edit a protected chapter or an `all`-protected label's block; a `statement`-protected block may only gain a `proof` environment. Wiring INTO protected blocks is fine — other declarations may `\uses{}` their labels — but their own `\uses{}` lists are the mathematician's; report missing edges there in "Notes for dispatcher" instead of editing.
103	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
104	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
105	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
106	
107	## Workflow
108	
109	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
110	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
111	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
112	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
113	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
114	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
115	7. Write your report.
116	
117	## Logging
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# DAG Walker Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Seed
128	<label>
129	
130	## Status
131	<COMPLETE — cone fully grounded | PARTIAL — genuine gaps remain (listed below)>
132	
133	## Cone before → after
134	- ∞ holes: <N before> → <M after>
135	- broken \uses: <N before> → <M after>
136	- blocks added: <count>;  \uses edges added: <count>
137	
138	## Blocks added / proofs written
139	- `\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` — <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>
140	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
141	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
142	
143	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
144	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
145	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
146	
147	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
148	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
149	
150	## References consulted
151	- `references/<file>.md` — <what verbatim content you took>
152	
153	## Notes for dispatcher
154	- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>
155	```
156	
157	## Return Value
158	
159	Your final assistant message: one line — `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL — <one-sentence outcome>` — plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.

49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

85	    letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop

90	      ≃ₗ[(R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u)] (R₀.obj (op X.unop) : Type u) := by
91	  letI := internalHomObjModule X.unop
92	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))
93	    (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (R₀ ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))

173	/-- **Leg-B atomic claim: the lax-monoidal unit `ε` of `restrictScalars` along the open-immersion
174	structure ring iso `(f.appIso W').inv` is an isomorphism.**  Its underlying map is the (bijective)

766	`F = M.val.presheaf`, `G = N.val.presheaf`, the presheaf of types
767	`presheafHom F G` (`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafHom`) sends an open `W` to the morphisms of

773	noncomputable def homLocalSection {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules} {ι : Type*}
774	    (U : ι → X.Opens) (f : ∀ i, M.restrict (U i).ι ⟶ N.restrict (U i).ι) (i : ι) :
775	    (CategoryTheory.presheafHom M.val.presheaf N.val.presheaf).obj (op (U i)) where
776	  app W :=
777	    haveI hle : W.unop.left ≤ U i := W.unop.hom.le
778	    haveI himg : (U i).ι ''ᵁ ((U i).ι ⁻¹ᵁ W.unop.left) = W.unop.left := by
779	      simp only [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf, Scheme.Opens.opensRange_ι]
780	      exact inf_eq_right.mpr hle
781	    M.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himg.symm)) ≫
782	      ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (f i).val).app (op ((U i).ι ⁻¹ᵁ W.unop.left)) ≫
783	      N.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himg))
784	  naturality := by
785	    intro A B φ
786	    have hBA : (unop B).left ≤ (unop A).left := ((Over.forget (U i)).map φ.unop).le
787	    let κ : (U i).ι ⁻¹ᵁ (unop B).left ⟶ (U i).ι ⁻¹ᵁ (unop A).left :=
788	      (Opens.map (U i).ι.base).map (homOfLE hBA)
789	    have himgA : (U i).ι ''ᵁ ((U i).ι ⁻¹ᵁ (unop A).left) = (unop A).left := by
790	      simp only [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf, Scheme.Opens.opensRange_ι]
791	      exact inf_eq_right.mpr (unop A).hom.le
792	    have himgB : (U i).ι ''ᵁ ((U i).ι ⁻¹ᵁ (unop B).left) = (unop B).left := by
793	      simp only [Scheme.Hom.image_preimage_eq_opensRange_inf, Scheme.Opens.opensRange_ι]
794	      exact inf_eq_right.mpr (unop B).hom.le
795	    -- naturality of the underlying ab-presheaf morphism of `f i`
796	    have hm := ((PresheafOfModules.toPresheaf _).map (f i).val).naturality κ.op
797	    -- the two thin-poset square edges agree (`Opens X` is a thin poset)
798	    have hsubM : ((Over.forget (U i)).map φ.unop).op ≫ eqToHom (congrArg op himgB.symm)
799	        = eqToHom (congrArg op himgA.symm) ≫ ((U i).ι.opensFunctor.map κ).op :=
800	      Subsingleton.elim _ _
801	    have hsubN : ((U i).ι.opensFunctor.map κ).op ≫ eqToHom (congrArg op himgB)
802	        = eqToHom (congrArg op himgA) ≫ ((Over.forget (U i)).map φ.unop).op :=
803	      Subsingleton.elim _ _
804	    -- M-side: the φ-restriction followed by the `eqToHom` is the `eqToHom` followed by `κ`
805	    have hML : M.val.presheaf.map ((Over.forget (U i)).map φ.unop).op ≫
806	          M.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himgB.symm))
807	        = M.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himgA.symm)) ≫
808	          (M.restrict (U i).ι).val.presheaf.map κ.op := by
809	      rw [(M.val.presheaf.map_comp _ _).symm, hsubM]
810	      exact M.val.presheaf.map_comp _ _
811	    -- N-side analogue
812	    have hNR : N.val.presheaf.map ((U i).ι.opensFunctor.map κ).op ≫
813	          N.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himgB))
814	        = N.val.presheaf.map (eqToHom (congrArg op himgA)) ≫
815	          N.val.presheaf.map ((Over.forget (U i)).map φ.unop).op := by
816	      rw [(N.val.presheaf.map_comp _ _).symm, hsubN]
817	      exact N.val.presheaf.map_comp _ _
818	    dsimp only [Functor.comp_map, Functor.op_map, Functor.op_obj, Functor.comp_obj]
819	    rw [← Category.assoc, hML]
820	    erw [Category.assoc, reassoc_of% hm, hNR]
821	    simp only [Category.assoc]
822	    rfl

826	morphism `F ⟶ G`.**  Since `⊤` is terminal in `Opens X`, the value of `presheafHom F G`

832	    (s : (CategoryTheory.presheafHom F G).obj (op ⊤)) : F ⟶ G :=
833	  CategoryTheory.presheafHomSectionsEquiv F G

835	      intro W W' e
836	      dsimp only

843	lemma topSectionToHom_app {X : TopCat.{u}}
844	    {F G : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ ⥤ Ab.{u}}
845	    (s : (CategoryTheory.presheafHom F G).obj (op ⊤)) (W : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X)ᵒᵖ) :

6109	  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
6110	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
6111	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
6112	  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
6113	  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
6114	  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
6115	  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
6116	  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
6117	  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
6118	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
6119	  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
6120	  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
6121	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
6122	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
6123	  \[
6124	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
6125	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
6126	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
6127	  \]
6128	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
6129	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
6130	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
6131	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
6132	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
6133	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
6134	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
6135	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
6136	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
6137	\end{lemma}
6138	
6139	\begin{proof}
6140	
6141	\leanok
6142	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
6143	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
6144	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
6145	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
6146	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
6147	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
6148	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
6149	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
6150	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
6151	  \(\iota\)).
6152	
6153	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
6154	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
6155	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
6156	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
6157	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
6158	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
6159	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
6160	  identification
6161	  \[
6162	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
6163	    \qquad (V \le U),
6164	  \]
6165	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
6166	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
6167	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
6168	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
6169	\end{proof}

6174	[The per-section dual transport \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\)]
6175	  \label{lem:slice_dual_transport}
6176	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sliceDualTransport}
6177	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
6178	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let
6179	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Write \(\alpha\) for the open-immersion
6180	  structure-presheaf natural transformation with components
6181	  \(\alpha_U = (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,U).\mathrm{inv}\), a \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)}-level
6182	  ring isomorphism, and \(\beta = \mathtt{whiskerRight}\,\alpha\,
6183	  (\mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\) for its
6184	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\)-level shadow, with component
6185	  \(\beta_V \colon \mathcal{O}_Y(V) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)
6186	  (\(fV := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(V)\)). Then for every open \(V \subseteq Y\) there is an
6187	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
6188	  \[
6189	    \bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V)
6190	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
6191	    \bigl(\mathtt{dual}\,((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V),
6192	  \]
6193	  realised as a single \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\) that packages \emph{both} leg (A)
6194	  --- the slice-Hom base-change reindexing across \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) --- and leg (B)
6195	  --- the unit codomain ring-iso swap. The naturality of the section family in \(W\) has
6196	  \emph{two} distinct parts: the underlying base-morphism uniqueness in
6197	  \((\mathtt{Over}\,V.\mathrm{unop})^{\mathrm{op}}\) is \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (a thin
6198	  poset has at most one inclusion between objects), but the accompanying equation of
6199	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module maps is a genuine, separate obligation: it is the
6200	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-ring iso
6201	  \(\beta_W\), which \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) does \emph{not} discharge.
6202	\end{lemma}
6203	
6204	\begin{proof}
6205	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
6206	  The isomorphism is produced from an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear equivalence between the
6207	  left-hand module --- the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module inherited from
6208	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) --- and the right-hand \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\),
6209	  assembled via \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.toModuleIso}\), with both module structures
6210	  identified explicitly.
6211	
6212	  \emph{Leg (A): the slice-Hom reindexing, built categorically.} A dual section
6213	  \(\varphi\) of the left-hand value is a morphism \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M.\mathtt{val} \to
6214	  \mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,\mathbf{1}_X\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\). Its image is the dual
6215	  section over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\) whose component at \(W \le V\) (with image
6216	  \(fW := f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W)\)) is the categorical image
6217	  \[
6218	    (\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,
6219	      \bigl(\varphi_{(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(W \le V)))}\bigr),
6220	  \]
6221	  i.e.\ the component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
6222	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), and then
6223	  post-composed with the leg-(B) codomain swap below.
6224	
6225	  \emph{Leg (B): the unit codomain ring-iso swap, via the lax-monoidal unit \(\varepsilon\).}
6226	  The codomain of the reindexed component is the restriction of scalars of the unit section,
6227	  which must be carried to the unit section over \(Y\). This is exactly the inverse of the
6228	  lax-monoidal unit comparison of the restriction-of-scalars functor,
6229	  \[
6230	    \mathtt{codomainMap} \;:=\;
6231	      \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\bigr),
6232	      \qquad g := (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W).\mathrm{inv}.\mathrm{hom},
6233	  \]
6234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualUnitRingSwap}
6235	  where \(g\) is taken at the \emph{\(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)} level. The unit \(\varepsilon\)
6236	  is invertible because \(g\) is a ring isomorphism
6237	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso},
6238	  so \(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,g)\) is an isomorphism. The presheaf-level
6239	  identification underlying the unit handling is the dual-of-unit isomorphism for the unit
6240	  section\lean{PresheafOfModules.dualUnitIsoGen}.
6241	
6242	  \emph{Inverse.} The inverse reindexing depends on a set-theoretic down-set fact: because
6243	  \(f\) is an open immersion, \(fV \subseteq \mathrm{range}\,f\), so every open
6244	  \(W'' \le fV\) already lies in the image of \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\), namely
6245	  \(W'' = f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}(f^{-1}W'')\). This is the open-image down-set coverage of an
6246	  \(\mathtt{IsOpenImmersion}\): an open contained in the (open) image of the immersion is the
6247	  functor-image of its own preimage. The project records it as the down-set identity
6248	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) for \(V \le W\) (the helper
6249	  \(\mathtt{image\_preimage\_of\_le}\)\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.image_preimage_of_le}),
6250	  and it is exactly the lemma the inverse reindexing relies on to define the components on the
6251	  whole down-set of \(fV\).
6252	
6253	  With this in hand the inverse \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Hom}\) mirrors the forward
6254	  component formula, with two changes: it uses \((f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom}\) in
6255	  place of its inverse, and its \(\varepsilon\)-codomain swap is
6256	  \(\mathtt{dualUnitRingSwapHom} = \mathtt{inv}\bigl(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,
6257	  (f.\mathtt{appIso}\,W'').\mathrm{hom})\bigr)\)\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dualUnitRingSwapHom}
6258	  --- that is, the \emph{inverse} of \(\varepsilon\) in the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction (where
6259	  \(\varepsilon\) is invertible by
6260	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_ε_restrictScalars_appIso_hom}), \emph{not}
6261	  \(\varepsilon\) of the \(\mathrm{inv}\)-direction \(\beta\). (The reverse reindex turns an
6262	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fW'')\)-section map into an \(\mathcal{O}_Y(W'')\)-map, so it must restrict
6263	  along the \(\mathrm{hom}\)-direction ring iso, whose codomain swap is therefore \(\mathtt{inv}\,
6264	  \varepsilon\) of \emph{that} functor.) The whole family is reindexed by the inverse of the
6265	  down-set bijection \(W'' \leftrightarrow f^{-1}W''\) above. The round-trip identities \(\mathtt{left\_inv}\)
6266	  and \(\mathtt{right\_inv}\) then close component-wise by \(\mathtt{Iso.inv\_hom\_id}\) and
6267	  \(\mathtt{Iso.hom\_inv\_id}\) of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) (the forward/inverse \(\beta\)-swaps
6268	  cancel) together with the cancellation of the down-set bijection
6269	  \(\iota_!(\iota^{-1}V) = V\) against its inverse.
6270	
6271	  For additivity and \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity, one must be careful about \emph{which}
6272	  module structure carries the left-hand value. The additive and scalar structure on a dual
6273	  section of the left-hand value
6274	  \(\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})\bigr)(V)\)
6275	  is the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module structure on the internal-hom object
6276	  (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}) --- the pointwise module structure in which the
6277	  sum and scalar multiple of dual sections are formed at the terminal object \(\mathrm{id}_{fV}\)
6278	  of the slice over \(fV\). This agrees with, but is \emph{a priori} a distinct presentation of,
6279	  the additive and scalar structure of the underlying presheaf morphism (the pointwise add and
6280	  scalar action on morphisms of presheaves of modules). The verification of additivity and
6281	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity of the transport therefore proceeds in three distinct steps, the
6282	  first of which is a required first move rather than an afterthought:
6283	  \begin{enumerate}
6284	    \item[(i)] \emph{Identify the two module presentations.} The internal-hom module structure's
6285	      addition and scalar action are identified with the underlying pointwise add and scalar action
6286	      on presheaf morphisms. This is a definitional identification of the two
6287	      \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module presentations of the same group of dual sections: the
6288	      internal-hom operations, formed at the terminal object of the slice, are precisely the
6289	      pointwise morphism-level operations of the presheaf morphism they package. Until this
6290	      identification is made, the additivity and scalar-compatibility obligations of the transport
6291	      cannot be read off the change-of-rings compatibility, because their sums and scalar multiples
6292	      are taken in the \emph{internal-hom} structure, not the morphism structure.
6293	    \item[(ii)] \emph{Match the X-side and Y-side scalars by the \(\beta\)-naturality ring
6294	      identity.} The scalar acting on the X-side (the pushforward-restricted scalar \(s\) by
6295	      which \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W\) multiplies the reindexed component) and the
6296	      scalar acting on the Y-side (the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-scalar \(c\) of the internal-hom
6297	      action) are \emph{a priori} elements of different rings and cannot be compared directly.
6298	      The bridge is the ring identity
6299	      \[
6300	        s \;=\; (\beta.\mathtt{app}\,W').\mathrm{hom}\, c,
6301	      \]
6302	      obtained from \(\mathtt{InternalHom.termRingMap\_naturality}\) --- which records how the
6303	      internal-hom term-ring map transports the scalar action across the slice --- together with
6304	      the naturality of \(\beta\) on the thin poset \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\). Without this identity
6305	      the two sides of the scalar-compatibility equation are not even of matching type, so it
6306	      must be supplied before the change-of-rings compatibility of step (iii) can be invoked.
6307	      The restricted scalar action is made explicit by
6308	      \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars.smul\_def'}\), which exhibits the restricted smul
6309	      as the original smul along \(\beta_W\).
6310	    \item[(iii)] \emph{Apply the change-of-rings compatibility on the morphism level.} Once the
6311	      operations are expressed pointwise on the underlying morphisms and the scalars matched by
6312	      (ii), additivity and
6313	      \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearity follow from the post-composition compatibility of the
6314	      change-of-rings reindexing: the functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W\) preserves sums
6315	      and scalar multiples of morphisms, and the structure scalar is intertwined by the ring
6316	      isomorphism \(\beta_W\). This last is the presheaf-level shadow of
6317	      \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}.
6318	  \end{enumerate}
6319	
6320	  \emph{Naturality of the section family in \(W\).} This splits into two obligations of
6321	  different character, and it is a mistake to discharge the whole square by
6322	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) alone. (a) The underlying \emph{base}-morphism naturality ---
6323	  that the two routes around a square agree on the \emph{indexing} morphisms of
6324	  \((\mathtt{Over}\,V.\mathrm{unop})^{\mathrm{op}}\) --- holds by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
6325	  since \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset and there is at most one inclusion between any
6326	  two objects; this discharges \emph{only} the uniqueness of the base morphisms. (b) The
6327	  actual naturality obligation, however, is an equation between
6328	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-\emph{module} maps, and it is \emph{not} settled by (a). Once the base
6329	  morphisms are pinned by (a), the module-map equation reduces to the \(\varepsilon\)-naturality
6330	  square of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) --- the naturality of the lax-monoidal unit
6331	  \(\varepsilon\) with respect to the restriction maps of \emph{both} the source and the target
6332	  presheaf, along the structure-ring isomorphism \(\beta_W\) --- post-composed with the
6333	  definition of the codomain swap \(\mathtt{dualUnitRingSwap} = \mathtt{inv}\,
6334	  \varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W)\). This \(\varepsilon\)-naturality step is a
6335	  genuine, separate obligation; the thin-poset argument of (a) does \emph{not} discharge it.
6336	  % NOTE: the Lean helper that delivers step (b) is
6337	  % `PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxε`.
6338	  The required \(\varepsilon\)-commutativity-with-restriction-maps square is supplied by the
6339	  natural transformation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLax}\varepsilon\), whose
6340	  components are the lax-monoidal units
6341	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.}\varepsilon(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,
6342	  (\alpha.\mathrm{app}\,X).\mathrm{hom})\): its \(\mathtt{NatTrans}\) naturality field is
6343	  \emph{exactly} the square asserting that \(\varepsilon\) commutes with the restriction maps
6344	  of the source and target presheaves along \(\beta_W\), which is what step (b) requires. The
6345	  prover invokes that naturality field directly; the thin-poset \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\)
6346	  of (a) settles only the base morphisms.
6347	\end{proof}

6354	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
6355	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
6356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
6357	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
6358	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
6359	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
6360	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
6361	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
6362	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
6363	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
6364	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
6365	  % then the canonical map
6366	  % $$
6367	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
6368	  % \longrightarrow

6368	  % \longrightarrow
6369	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
6370	  % $$
6371	  % is an isomorphism."
6372	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
6373	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
6374	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
6375	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
6376	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
6377	  \[
6378	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
6379	  \]
6380	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
6381	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
6382	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
6383	\end{lemma}
6384	
6385	\begin{proof}
6386	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv,
6387	        lem:slice_dual_transport}
6388	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
6389	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
6390	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
6391	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
6392	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
6393	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
6394	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
6395	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
6396	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
6397	
6398	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
6399	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
6400	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
6401	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
6402	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
6403	  \[
6404	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
6405	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
6406	  \]
6407	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
6408	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
6409	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
6410	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
6411	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
6412	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
6413	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
6414	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
6415	
6416	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
6417	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
6418	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
6419	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
6420	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
6421	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
6422	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
6423	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
6424	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
6425	  \emph{exactly}
6426	  \[
6427	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
6428	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
6429	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
6430	  \]
6431	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
6432	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
6433	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
6434	  two-leg split of the whole step.
6435	
6436	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
6437	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
6438	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
6439	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
6440	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
6441	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
6442	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
6443	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
6444	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
6445	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
6446	
6447	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
6448	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
6449	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
6450	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
6451	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
6452	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
6453	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
6454	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
6455	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
6456	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
6457	
6458	  \emph{The combined leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Both legs are
6459	  realised together by a single \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism
6460	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), built
6461	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
6462	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion
6463	  compares the restriction functors and the units, but carries \emph{no} internal-hom or
6464	  dual content; the assertion realized by leg (A) --- that the dual commutes with slice
6465	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens
6466	  (\(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\)) --- cannot be supplied by such an object without a
6467	  monoidal-closed structure on sheaves of modules, which this development deliberately does
6468	  \emph{not} carry (cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The atom must
6469	  therefore be constructed directly.
6470	
6471	  Concretely (\cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}), the forward component of
6472	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) at \(W \le V\) is the categorical image
6473	  \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,(\varphi_{\bullet})\) of the dual-section
6474	  component of \(\varphi\) at the \(f\)-image of \(W\), reindexed across
6475	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), post-composed with
6476	  the leg-(B) codomain swap \(\mathtt{inv}(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W))\).
6477	  Leg (A) is built categorically via \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\):
6478	  a genuine change-of-rings functor acts here, so the reindexing must be applied as a
6479	  functor map rather than pointwise. Leg (B)'s unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) is invertible
6480	  because \(\beta_W\) is a ring isomorphism, as detailed in \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
6481	  Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
6482	  naturality squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The forward and inverse maps
6483	  identify the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
6484	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.
6485	
6486	  The Step-4 residual is then \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\) applied \emph{directly} to
6487	  the family \(V \mapsto \mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) --- which already packages
6488	  \emph{both} legs (A) and (B) --- with the outer naturality square thin-poset-trivial. No
6489	  separate leg-(B) step is interposed in \(\mathtt{isoMk}\): the per-section atom of
6490	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} carries the full leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) content. The outer
6491	  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) naturality square follows from the per-section family naturality
6492	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport.naturality}\) of \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}: the
6493	  \(\varepsilon\)-naturality is established first at the level of individual sections, and the
6494	  outer presheaf-morphism naturality of \(\mathtt{isoMk}\) then follows.
6495	
6496	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
6497	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
6498	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
6499	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
6500	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
6501	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
6502	
6503	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
6504	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
6505	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
6506	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
6507	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
6508	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
6509	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
6510	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
6511	
6512	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
6513	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
6514	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
6515	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
6516	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
6517	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
6518	
6519	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
6520	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
6521	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
6522	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
6523	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
6524	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
6525	
6526	  \emph{The inverse-uniqueness shortcut is not viable.} One might hope to derive the
6527	  isomorphism from the already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of
6528	  monoidal inverses --- the dual being the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and
6529	  restriction commuting with \(\otimes\) --- sidestepping leg (A). The abstract statements of
6530	  this idiom (\(\mathtt{hasRightDualOfEquivalence}\), \(\mathtt{rightDualIso}\)) require,
6531	  however, four structures the present setting lacks: a registered \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}\)
6532	  instance on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) (the monoidal structure here is carried entirely
6533	  by hand), a \emph{strong} (not merely lax/oplax) monoidal restriction functor, restriction
6534	  being an \emph{equivalence} (false for a non-surjective open immersion, which is a
6535	  localization), and an exact pairing \(\mathtt{ExactPairing}\,M\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M)\) (which
6536	  exists only for dualizable \(M\), whereas the lemma is stated for general \(M\)). Even
6537	  restricted to the invertible consumer this would require building a coevaluation and
6538	  verifying the zig-zag identities --- strictly \emph{more} infrastructure than leg (A) --- so
6539	  the construction commits to legs (A) and (B) above.
6540	\end{proof}

name: ts302-leandag-statement-uses-only
description: leandag DAG edges come from STATEMENT-level \uses only; proof-block \uses are NOT traversed. Put construction ingredients in the statement \uses.
metadata:
  type: project
---

In this project's `leandag` config, the dependency DAG is built from **statement-level
`\uses` ONLY**. A `\uses{}` placed inside a `\begin{proof}…\end{proof}` is **not** a
traversable edge: `lem:dual_restrict_iso` showed `dep_count = 2` (its statement uses)
despite its proof `\uses{lem:slice_dual_transport}`, and `slice_dual_transport` was absent
from `dual_restrict_iso`'s `archon dag-query ancestors`.

**How to apply:** when wiring a helper into a consumer so it lands on the cone, add the edge
to the consumer's **statement** `\uses` (adding to the proof too is fine/harmless but does
nothing for the graph). The project convention already does this — e.g.
`lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial`'s statement `\uses` its 3-step construction ingredients
(`dual_restrict_iso`, `dual_unit_iso`, `dualIsoOfIso`). So "construction ingredient of a
proof" belongs in the STATEMENT `\uses` here.

**Why:** explains the [[ts297-goal-cone-massively-underwired]] symptom mechanically — many
nodes are "off-cone" simply because their consumers reference them only in proof `\uses`.
Promoting the key ingredient into the statement `\uses` reconnects them (iter-302: promoting
`slice_dual_transport` into `dual_restrict_iso`'s statement `\uses` pulled the whole dual
cluster into `dual_isLocallyTrivial`'s 35-node ancestor cone).

Also (iter-302): inline `\lean{X}` inside a proof body is NOT counted as coverage for `X`
(leandag pins X to the FIRST `\lean` of a `\label`'d block); such helpers show as
`archon dag-query unmatched`. Fix = give each its own labeled block; convert the now-redundant
inline `\lean{X}` to `\cref{label}` so X is pinned exactly once.
